# The Biggest STEM Hiring Trends of 2023

![woman working on electronics](https://stemhunter.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/pexels-rfstudio-3825582.jpg "pexels-rfstudio-3825582")

In recent years, the field of STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics) has experienced [rapid growth and innovation](https://stemhunter.com/these-are-the-stem-jobs-of-the-future/). As we enter 2023, it is essential to understand the biggest hiring trends shaping the industry. From the role of technology to the push for diversity and remote work, this article explores the key factors that will impact STEM hiring in the coming year.

## Understanding the STEM Job Market in 2023

As technology continues to evolve, so does the demand for skilled professionals in STEM fields. In 2023, it is crucial to be aware of the changes taking place in the job market.

The STEM job market in 2023 is a dynamic and ever-changing landscape. With advancements in technology and the increasing reliance on automation, the hiring process for STEM positions has undergone significant transformations. Employers are now utilizing automated systems and artificial intelligence (AI) to streamline recruitment, making it essential for job seekers to be well-versed in using technology and showcasing their skills online.

However, it’s not just the use of technology in the hiring process that job seekers need to be aware of. Emerging technologies like blockchain and virtual reality are creating new opportunities in STEM fields. Professionals who can adapt to these technologies and stay ahead of the curve will have a competitive advantage in the job market. Whether it’s understanding the intricacies of blockchain technology or being able to develop immersive virtual reality experiences, staying updated with these emerging trends will be crucial for success in the STEM job market.

### The Impact of Global Trends on STEM Jobs

In addition to technological advancements, global trends also play a significant role in shaping the STEM job market in 2023. As the world becomes more interconnected, employers are seeking professionals with a global mindset and cultural competency. Understanding how global trends, such as climate change or advancements in healthcare, affect STEM fields is crucial for job seekers.

For example, the [increasing focus on sustainability and renewable energy](https://stemhunter.com/the-fastest-growing-stem-fields-and-the-most-in-demand-jobs/) has led to a surge in demand for professionals with expertise in environmental science, renewable energy engineering, and sustainable design. Job seekers who can demonstrate their understanding of these global trends and showcase their ability to contribute to sustainable solutions will be highly sought after.

Moreover, the COVID-19 pandemic has had a profound impact on various industries, accelerating the digital transformation process. This has resulted in an increased demand for professionals with expertise in cybersecurity, data analytics, and remote work capabilities. As companies adapt to remote work environments and prioritize data security, job seekers who can demonstrate their ability to navigate these new challenges will be in high demand.

In conclusion, the STEM job market in 2023 is influenced by various factors, including technological advancements, emerging trends, and global changes. Job seekers must not only be proficient in utilizing technology but also stay updated with emerging technologies and understand how global trends impact their respective fields. By continuously learning and adapting, professionals can position themselves for success in the ever-evolving STEM job market.

## The Most In-Demand STEM Jobs in 2023

While the overall STEM job market is growing, certain roles will be particularly sought after in 2023. Job seekers should pay attention to these in-demand positions and tailor their skills accordingly.

In this article, we will explore the top STEM jobs that will be in high demand in 2023 and discuss the skills and qualifications job seekers should focus on to stand out in these fields.

### The Rise of Data Science and AI Jobs

Data science and [artificial intelligence](https://stemhunter.com/future-of-ai-jobs/) continue to dominate the STEM job market. As companies rely more on data to drive decision-making, professionals with expertise in data analytics and AI will be highly sought after.

Job seekers should consider acquiring skills in machine learning, programming languages, and data visualization to stand out in these fields. Additionally, demonstrating the ability to apply ethical considerations and address bias in machine learning models will be a significant advantage.

Data scientists will play a crucial role in extracting valuable insights from vast amounts of data, enabling companies to make data-driven decisions. They will develop and implement algorithms, build predictive models, and analyze complex data sets to identify patterns and trends.

Artificial intelligence specialists, on the other hand, will focus on developing intelligent systems and algorithms that can perform tasks that typically require human intelligence. They will work on projects such as natural language processing, computer vision, and machine learning algorithms.

As the demand for data science and AI professionals continues to rise, job seekers should also stay updated with the latest advancements in these fields. Keeping up with emerging technologies and industry trends will give them a competitive edge in the job market.

### The Growing Need for Environmental Scientists

With the increasing concern for [climate change and sustainability, environmental science](https://stemhunter.com/jobs-you-can-do-with-an-environmental-science-degree/) is becoming a critical area of focus. Professionals who can analyze and mitigate the environmental impact of industries will be in high demand in 2023.

Skills such as environmental modeling, renewable energy expertise, and knowledge of environmental regulations will be highly valued. Job seekers should showcase their passion for environmental stewardship and their ability to propose innovative solutions for sustainability challenges.

Environmental scientists will work on projects related to pollution control, environmental impact assessments, and resource management. They will conduct fieldwork, collect and analyze samples, and develop strategies to minimize the negative impact of human activities on the environment.

Job seekers interested in pursuing a career in environmental science should also consider specializing in areas such as climate change, water resource management, or sustainable development. Gaining practical experience through internships or research projects will further enhance their credentials and increase their chances of securing a job in this field.

Furthermore, professionals in this field should stay updated with the latest research and advancements in environmental science. They should be familiar with emerging technologies and innovative approaches to address environmental challenges.

In conclusion, the STEM job market in 2023 will be highly competitive, but job seekers who focus on developing the right skills and qualifications will have a better chance of securing in-demand positions. Data science and AI jobs will continue to dominate the market, while the growing need for environmental scientists reflects the increasing concern for sustainability. By staying updated with industry trends and showcasing their expertise, job seekers can position themselves for success in these thriving STEM fields.

## The Skills Employers are Looking for in 2023

Beyond specific job roles, employers in STEM fields are increasingly prioritizing certain skillsets when evaluating candidates. These skills encompass both technical expertise and soft skills.

In today’s rapidly evolving job market, it is no longer enough for candidates to possess only technical skills. Employers are now recognizing the importance of soft skills in the STEM industry. While technical skills are essential, soft skills are becoming increasingly valuable in the STEM job market. Employers are seeking candidates who can effectively collaborate, communicate, and adapt in diverse teams.

Job seekers should highlight their ability to think critically, solve problems, and demonstrate leadership. These skills are highly sought after by employers as they contribute to a positive work environment and promote innovation. Additionally, showing a willingness to engage in lifelong learning and professional development will set candidates apart from their peers.

Technical skills remain a crucial aspect of STEM hiring. In 2023, specific technical competencies will stand out among job applicants. Proficiency in programming languages, such as Python, Java, or R, is highly desirable across various STEM fields. These languages are widely used in software development, data analysis, and machine learning. Additionally, skills in cybersecurity, cloud computing, and data analysis will continue to be in high demand.

Employers are seeking candidates who can not only write code but also understand the importance of data security and privacy. With the increasing reliance on cloud computing, professionals who can effectively manage and secure data in the cloud will be highly sought after. Furthermore, the ability to analyze and interpret data is crucial in making informed decisions and driving innovation in STEM fields.

It is important for job seekers to stay updated with the latest trends and advancements in their respective fields. Employers value candidates who are proactive in expanding their knowledge and skills. By staying informed about emerging technologies and industry best practices, candidates can position themselves as valuable assets to potential employers.

In conclusion, the job market in 2023 will require a combination of technical expertise and soft skills. Employers are looking for candidates who can not only excel in their technical roles but also contribute to a collaborative and innovative work environment. By highlighting both their technical competencies and soft skills, job seekers can increase their chances of securing employment in the competitive STEM industry.

## The Future of Remote Work in STEM Fields

The COVID-19 pandemic has forever changed the way we work, including in STEM fields. Remote work is likely to become a permanent feature of the employment landscape, and professionals need to adapt to this new reality.

### The Benefits and Challenges of Remote STEM Jobs

Remote work offers flexibility and potential cost savings for both employers and employees. It allows companies to access a larger talent pool and enables professionals to achieve a better work-life balance.

However, remote work also presents challenges, such as maintaining communication and collaboration within teams. Job seekers should highlight their ability to work independently, use online collaboration tools effectively, and adapt to virtual work environments.

### Adapting to a Digital Work Environment

The transition to remote work means that professionals need to be proficient in digital tools and technologies. Familiarity with video conferencing platforms, project management software, and online collaboration tools will be essential in 2023.

Adapting to a digital work environment also requires strong organizational and time management skills. Job seekers who can demonstrate their ability to manage projects independently and meet deadlines in a virtual setting will be highly valued by employers.

## Diversity and Inclusion in STEM Hiring

Promoting [diversity and inclusion](https://stemhunter.com/company/diversity-in-stem/) has been a growing focus in the STEM industry, and this trend will continue in 2023. Employers are recognizing the value that diverse perspectives bring to innovation and problem-solving.

### The Push for More Women in STEM

Efforts to encourage women to pursue STEM careers will remain a priority in 2023. Companies are actively working to eliminate barriers and promote gender equality in the workplace.

Job seekers who can demonstrate their commitment to advancing gender diversity and equality will be highly sought after. Additionally, being part of professional networks or organizations that support women in STEM will enhance job prospects.

### Encouraging Ethnic Diversity in STEM Fields

Alongside gender diversity, ethnic diversity is also a significant focus in STEM hiring. Employers are seeking candidates from diverse backgrounds who can contribute unique perspectives and ideas.

Job seekers should highlight their multicultural experiences, language skills, and involvement in initiatives that promote ethnic diversity in STEM. Demonstrating an understanding of cultural nuances and inclusivity in the workplace will be highly valued.

In conclusion, 2023 will bring significant hiring trends to the STEM industry. As technology continues to shape the job market, job seekers should adapt their skills to meet the demand for data science, environmental science, and other in-demand careers. Additionally, soft skills, remote work capabilities, and diversity and inclusion efforts are critical factors to consider in the competitive STEM job market. By staying informed and showcasing their unique qualifications, professionals can position themselves for success in the ever-evolving STEM landscape.

# Why STEM Education Matters in Today’s Competitive Job Market

![Why STEM Education Matters in Today’s Competitive Job Market](https://deekshastemschools.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Why-STEM-Education-Matters-in-Todays-Competitive-Job-Market.webp "Why STEM Education Matters in Today’s Competitive Job Market")

In today’s fast-evolving world, the job market is increasingly driven by advancements in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM). From artificial intelligence to renewable energy and biotechnology, industries are rapidly adopting STEM innovations, creating a demand for skilled professionals. For example, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics projects a 22% increase in computer and information technology jobs by 2030, underscoring the growing need for expertise in STEM fields. This blog explores the significance of STEM education in equipping students with the skills needed to thrive in this competitive landscape.

## **The Rising Demand for STEM Professionals**

### **Transforming Industries**

STEM fields are at the forefront of innovation, transforming industries such as healthcare, finance, and transportation. For example, in healthcare, machine learning algorithms are being used to predict patient outcomes and optimize treatment plans. In finance, blockchain technology is revolutionizing secure transactions, and in transportation, autonomous vehicles are reshaping mobility and logistics. For instance, data science and artificial intelligence are revolutionizing decision-making processes, while advancements in renewable energy are addressing global environmental challenges. This insight complements the broader transformation of industries like healthcare, finance, and transportation, where STEM-driven innovation is reshaping traditional paradigms.

### **Job Growth and Opportunities**

According to global employment statistics, STEM careers are among the fastest-growing and highest-paying professions. Roles like software developers, data analysts, and engineers are in high demand, and this trend is expected to continue in the coming decades.

## **How STEM Education Prepares Students for the Future**

### **Problem-Solving and Critical Thinking**

STEM education emphasizes analytical thinking and innovative problem-solving. For instance, students might participate in activities like designing eco-friendly water filtration systems or programming robots to navigate complex mazes, which challenge them to apply theoretical concepts to practical problems. Students learn to tackle real-world challenges, such as designing sustainable solutions or optimizing technological processes.

### **Technological Proficiency**

In an age dominated by digital tools, STEM education ensures students are adept at using and understanding advanced technologies, from programming languages to robotics and automation.

### **Adaptability and Lifelong Learning**

STEM instills a mindset of continuous learning, enabling individuals to adapt to ever-changing technologies and market demands.

## **Benefits of STEM Education in the Job Market**

### **Higher Employability**

Candidates with a STEM background are often preferred by employers due to their technical expertise and problem-solving capabilities. A recent survey by the National Association of Colleges and Employers (NACE) revealed that 72% of employers actively seek candidates with strong STEM skills, highlighting the value of this educational foundation.

### **Competitive Salaries**

STEM careers typically offer lucrative salaries and growth opportunities, making them attractive options for young professionals.

### **Global Opportunities**

STEM education opens doors to global career opportunities, as the skills gained are universally applicable across industries and regions.

## **Why Deeksha STEM Schools Are the Right Choice**

### **Focused STEM Curriculum**

Deeksha STEM Schools integrate practical learning and innovative teaching methodologies to prepare students for STEM careers. For example, their “Tech Creators Club” allows students to develop real-world projects like mobile apps and IoT devices, fostering both technical expertise and innovation. From coding workshops to science fairs, students gain hands-on experience.

### **Industry-Relevant Skills**

The curriculum is designed to align with industry trends, ensuring students are equipped with the skills needed for success in competitive job markets.

### **Holistic Development**

Beyond academics, Deeksha STEM Schools emphasize teamwork, communication, and ethical practices, creating well-rounded individuals ready for professional challenges.

## **Conclusion**

STEM education is not just about learning technical subjects; it’s about fostering creativity, resilience, and adaptability—qualities essential for success in today’s competitive job market. At Deeksha STEM Schools, we are committed to empowering students with a strong STEM foundation, preparing them for a future full of possibilities. 2024 STEM Job Growth Index (STEMdex)
====================================

![Image 2](https://www.rclco.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/AdobeStock_213567841-scaled-e1733862822526.webp)

**December 12, 2024**

By[Gregg Logan, Managing Director](https://www.rclco.com/leadership/gregg-logan/),[Ryan Guerdan, Vice President](https://www.rclco.com/leadership/ryan-guerdan/)

[Download Publication](https://www.rclco.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/RCLCO-Advisory-2024-STEM-Job-Growth-Index.pdf)

Since 2016, RCLCO has published the STEMdex, an annual analytical ranking of U.S. metropolitan areas that evaluates their potential for future growth in Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM) industries. These rankings identify markets with the strongest prospects for STEM employment and industry expansion, providing valuable insights for stakeholders across sectors.

STEM industries are vital drivers of innovation and economic growth. These jobs are among the highest-paying and most impactful, attracting a diverse, highly educated workforce. Nationwide from 2019 to 2023, employment in STEM roles grew by 2%, outpacing the 0.9% growth in non-STEM jobs. Salaries in STEM fields averaged $112,800 annually, nearly double the $61,600 average for non-STEM roles.([1](https://www.rclco.com/publication/2024-stem-job-growth-index-stemdex/#1))

RCLCO’s STEMdex rankings focus on the 50 largest Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSAs), which account for nearly 70% of U.S. STEM jobs and 60% of all employment nationwide. The analysis forecasts the concentration of STEM job growth over the next five to ten years, making it a critical tool for policy makers, urban planners, real estate developers, and corporate leaders.

The Strategic Importance of STEMdex
-----------------------------------

RCLCO has been a leading advisor on property investment, planning, and real estate development for nearly six decades. The firm’s work is deeply impacted by the growth of the STEM industry, which drives demand for housing, office space, and infrastructure. The STEMdex model evaluates 24 factors, including economic trends, workforce quality, tax environments, and quality of life metrics, to predict where STEM industries will flourish. These insights inform decisions on municipal policies, talent acquisition strategies, and urban development plans.

### Trends Driving STEM Job Growth

Over the past five years, Professional Services, Information, and Financial Services have driven the most significant gains in STEM jobs, collectively adding 180,000 roles annually. While STEM employment in Manufacturing and Natural Resources has declined([2](https://www.rclco.com/publication/2024-stem-job-growth-index-stemdex/#2)), emerging technologies such as Artificial Intelligence (AI) and green technology are creating new opportunities:

*   **Artificial Intelligence:** AI is revolutionizing STEM fields by enabling faster research and development, enhancing automation, and creating new job categories. AI-driven sectors are experiencing 4.8 times greater labor productivity compared to those without AI exposure. Notably, job postings in generative AI rose by 750% between April 2022 and April 2024([3](https://www.rclco.com/publication/2024-stem-job-growth-index-stemdex/#3)). AI advancements, such as streamlining drug discovery processes([4](https://www.rclco.com/publication/2024-stem-job-growth-index-stemdex/#4)), highlight its transformative impact across industries.
*   **Green Technology:** Climate resilience and sustainability efforts are fueling demand for STEM roles in renewable energy, green manufacturing, and sustainable infrastructure. Positions such as renewable energy engineers and battery technicians are increasingly sought after as governments and businesses prioritize combating climate change.

“STEM jobs, driven by a highly educated workforce, are among the highest-paying and most economically impactful roles in any market. Their significance to the economy is immense, and our STEMdex, now in its eighth edition, is designed to spotlight markets with robust industry employment today and those poised for significant growth in the future. The STEMdex offers a straightforward and intuitive tool to help people grasp the employment and housing trends that STEM job growth will bring to these cities in the coming years.”

– _**Gregg Logan**, Managing Director, RCLCO_

### Challenges Amid Tech Layoffs

Despite strong overall growth, the tech sector has faced headline-grabbing layoffs. Between 2022 and 2024, 554,000 employees of tech firms were laid off due to factors such as rising interest rates, inflation, and shifts in venture capital priorities. However, much of the downsizing affected non-technical roles, including HR and communications, which often transition to other industries. The long-term outlook for STEM jobs remains positive as technology adoption and innovation continue to expand.

The city also ranks in the top five for quality-of-life factors, benefiting from its cultural vibrancy and outdoor amenities. However, challenges such as high office costs and limited airport connectivity could impact its future competitiveness.

### Rising Stars and Notable Shifts

Phoenix entered the Top 20 rankings this year, replacing Philadelphia. Population growth, a diversifying economy, and relatively lower office costs have bolstered Phoenix’s position as a rising STEM hub. Conversely, Philadelphia’s drop reflects challenges in economic momentum and affordability, which are critical drivers of competitiveness.

The 2024 STEMdex highlights the dynamic nature of metro area performance, influenced by shifting economic conditions, workforce trends, and lifestyle preferences. Austin’s sustained leadership underscores the importance of balancing economic vitality, workforce quality, and quality of life. Meanwhile, the rise of metros like Phoenix demonstrate the power of favorable cost structures and demographic trends in propelling emerging markets.

For businesses, governments, and developers, the STEMdex provides a roadmap to identify opportunities, anticipate challenges, and align strategies with the future of STEM industry growth. As the STEM sector continues to drive innovation, these insights will be invaluable for shaping the next generation of cities and economies.

### Methodology

RCLCO analyzed 24 metrics from various sources, including the US Census Bureau, Bureau of Labor Statistics, Oxford Economics, Numbeo, The Tax Foundation, Costar, and the Federal Aviation Administration. These metrics fall into four categories and are weighted based on their historical correlation to STEM employment growth. The categories include:

STEM Trends:

Examines population growth, industry-specific growth, and STEM wages

Workforce Quality:

Evaluates educational attainment and millennial/Gen-Z population factors

Quality of Life:

Incorporates cost of living, walk scores, and commute times

Business Climate:

Considers factors such as office costs and the local tax environment. Notably, while Business Climate inversely correlates with current STEM employment concentrations, it remains a significant factor for firms and workers evaluating relocation or expansion opportunities

To determine the weights for each category, RCLCO reviewed past results of the STEMdex and STEM employment growth in the studied markets. This analysis demonstrates that markets with a high concentration of employment in a specific STEM industry tend to attract further investment from new firms. This is largely due to these markets’ access to skilled talent, existing logistical infrastructure, and desirable lifestyle amenities. Consequently, factors related to established STEM concentrations and robust economic infrastructure receive a high weighting in the model, as they are likely to continue to fuel job growth in the sector. The other highest weighted factors relate to what will attract future talent such as access to education, commute mode variety, and home prices.

Currently, the highest concentrations of STEM employment in the U.S. are found in cities like San Jose (Silicon Valley), Washington, D.C., San Francisco, Austin, and Seattle. Other top markets include Boston, Raleigh, and Denver, which benefit from young, educated populations. Quality of life remains a crucial factor for families and workers choosing where to live and work, with hybrid and remote employment emerging as key drivers of migration. Knowledge-based jobs, which often allow for remote work, have contributed to suppressed office attendance in high-cost markets while driving growth in more affordable cities offering similar lifestyle amenities.

Despite current job concentrations being a critical consideration, RCLCO designed the STEMdex to forecast where growth will occur over the next five to ten years. By balancing factors like emerging trends, workforce quality, quality of life, and business climate, the model predicts which markets are best positioned for future STEM employment expansion. This forward-looking approach ensures that the STEMdex remains a valuable tool for anticipating shifts in the evolving STEM landscape.

### Implications for Policymakers and Businesses

Policymakers and local stakeholders can use employment projections as a means of aligning education, infrastructure, and economic development. Infrastructure policies informed by employment projections can help support future economic needs. For example, growth in logistics or e-commerce may require investments in transportation networks, broadband expansion, or smart city initiatives. Similarly, rising demand for clean energy jobs could guide investments in renewable energy infrastructure, such as solar farms and EV charging networks – a significant consideration in land allocation decisions. Additionally, investments in affordable housing, public transit, and relocation incentives can enhance workforce mobility in response to regional job growth. Economic policies can use employment projections to incentivize high-growth sectors and address regional disparities. Identifying industries poised for expansion, such as biotech, green energy, or AI, allows policymakers to target tax incentives, subsidies, or grants to stimulate these areas.

April Jobs Report: 175k Jobs Added, Marking 40th Consecutive Month of Employment Growth
=======================================================================================

The Bureau of Labor Statistics released the April 2024 Jobs Report and we're taking a look at the numbers and trends impacting our industries.
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

03 May 2024 5 minutes

![Image 11: digital-landscape-gettyimages-1264838897](https://www.specialiststaffinggroup.com/media/1bmjv4xi/digital-landscape-gettyimages-1264838897.jpg?width=1600&height=750&v=1db7e06ccc61a00)

The Bureau of Labor Statistics released April's Jobs Report showing 175k jobs were added overall and unemployment now sits at 3.9%.The US unemployment rate has stayed below 4% for more than two years,a remarkable streak that hasn't been matched in decades.While this isn't the growth analysts had predicted (they had expected to see 232k jobs added and to unemployment remain steady at 3.8%), it's still a positive report overall.

So far this year, the economy has added, on average, 276,000 jobs per month, Bureau of Labor Statistics data shows. That’s about 25,000 more jobs per month than last year and 111,000 more per month than in 2019. With today's numbers we are in now the 40th consecutive month of employment expansion, the fifth longest on record, and the 26th month in a row that the nation’s jobless rate held below 4%. That nearly matches a 27-month streak from 1967 to 1970.Economists, taking this report alongside the current interest rate into consideration, predict we will now start to see a gradual, fairly orderly slowdown in the labor market moving forward.

In the industries we support, we continue to see STEM positions fueling new job growth. Here's a look at industry performance in April.

Industry Performance
--------------------

### Tech + Life Sciences:

Professional, Scientific and Technical services which includes both tech and life sciences subdivisions added a total of 17.1k jobs. The big news is the big drop in unemployment rate month-over-month. The industry now has an unemployment rate of 3.0% compared to 3.4% one month ago.

### Engineering/Manufacturing:

Engineering and manufacturing saw a jump in job additions this month with a total of 8K jobs added. As is with tech and life sciences, the big news here is the unemployment rate in the industry dropped to 2.7% the lowest it's been since August of last year and lower than average.

### Finance:

6k jobs were added in the finance sector last month compared to 3k added in the month prior. This industry continues to build momentum in terms of job additions as the year progresses.

### Marketing:

Marketing saw an uptick in jobs month-over-month, adding 2.5k jobs, slightly up compared to the 900 jobs added in the industry in March.

Where STEM Hiring is Happening
------------------------------

The U.S. is experiencing significant growth in STEM jobs, fueled by advancements in technology and digital transformation across industries. From software development to cybersecurity, data analysis, and artificial intelligence, the demand for skilled tech professionals continues to soar. An analysis of U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics data shows significant job growth in the Professional and Business Services sector, which includes the professional, scientific and technical services subsector. More workers are added to payrolls in not just states with large tech hubs, but across other smaller states poised for economic growth in the tech industry.

Top States Adding Professional & Business Services Workers

*   Georgia - 6,200
*   *New York - 5,300
*   California - 5,200
*   Arizona - 2,600
*   Colorado - 2,100
*   *Texas - 1,700
*   Arkansas - 1,400

_Source: [BLS (February to March 2024)](https://www.bls.gov/news.release/laus.t03.htm)

*Notes state with Computer Futures office_

The Future of Work
------------------

The [BLS](https://www.bls.gov/emp/tables/stem-employment.htm) recently updated their projections for STEM occupational growth in the U.S. through 2032. Growth in the number of jobs in STEM is now at 10.8%, revised upward from previous calculations, and much higher than non-STEM job growth. This means professionals who possess high-level STEM skills will continue to be in-demand.

However, despite the high demand for this talent, we're still seeing a persistent skills gap, fueled by rapid technological advancements, evolving job requirements, and disparities in education and training. Many employers turn to specialized recruitment firms after their struggle to find qualified candidates to fill open positions.

To address the skills gap and meet the growing demand for STEM talent, we're seeing a rise in investment across sectors in initiatives to promote STEM education, training, and workforce development. This includes partnerships between industry, academia, and government to provide hands-on learning opportunities, apprenticeships, and upskilling programs. Furthermore, efforts to enhance diversity, equity, and inclusion in STEM fields are gaining traction, as organizations recognize the importance of diverse perspectives and talent in driving innovation and problem-solving.

Overall, while the demand for STEM talent in the US remains strong, addressing the skills gap and fostering a diverse and inclusive STEM workforce are essential for driving continued innovation, economic prosperity, and global competitiveness.

Labor Market Trends and Considerations for STEM Graduate Education
==================================================================

[Essay/Brief](https://www.equityinhighered.org/resources/ideas-and-insights#essays-briefs)

[Download the Essay (PDF)](https://www.equityinhighered.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/REHE-STEM-Lab-LaborMarketTrends.pdf)

**By Lindsay Johnson**

The U.S. economy is at a pivotal moment. The rapidly changing nature of technology puts increased emphasis on science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) fields and the need for STEM skillsets across workplaces and regions. Not surprisingly, data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) shows that the number of STEM occupations is on the rise. BLS’s Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics data projected a 10.8 percent increase by 2032, compared with a 2.3 percent increase in non-STEM occupations. Notably, these occupations offer a median annual wage of $101,650, compared with that of $46,680 among non-STEM occupations. The wage potential alone makes a compelling case for many Americans to consider a career in STEM, whether weighing short-term professional growth, long-term career stability, or the return on investment of an advanced degree.

This brief is a companion to _STEM Graduate Education: Trends and Existing Interventions to Broaden STEM Graduate Pathways_, which provides a snapshot of enrollment and completion trends in STEM education and offers an overview of existing interventions to increase the number of diverse graduates entering the labor force. This brief summarizes the major trends in the STEM labor market that influence STEM graduate education and provides supporting quantitative data. It concludes with considerations for higher education institutions and related stakeholders interested in better preparing graduate students for in-demand STEM jobs.

enriched_points.expanded_content: The STEM job market is rapidly transforming, driven by advancements in AI, data science, and green technology, leading to increased demand for skilled professionals in these fields.
As technologies evolve, professionals must adapt quickly, learning new skills and technologies such as blockchain and virtual reality to remain competitive in the job market.
Employers seek candidates with strong soft skills like collaboration, communication, and critical thinking to complement their technical expertise, fostering innovation and effective teamwork.
